(Romanian pronunciation: [[ˈardʒeʃ piˈteʃtʲ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian")), **Argeș Pitești** or simply **FC Argeș**, is a Romanian professional [football club](/wiki/Football_club "Football club") based in [Pitești](/wiki/Pite%C8%99ti "Pitești"), [Argeș County](/wiki/Arge%C8%99_County "Argeș County"), that competes in the [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"), the top tier of [Romanian football](/wiki/Romanian_football_league_system "Romanian football league system").

Association football club in Pitești

This article is about the original club. For the fan-owned phoenix team founded in 2013 and dissolved in 2016, see [FC Argeș 1953 Pitești](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_1953_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș 1953 Pitești").

**Asociația Clubul Sportiv Campionii Fotbal Club Argeș**, commonly known as **FC Argeș Pitești** (Romanian pronunciation: [[ˈardʒeʃ piˈteʃtʲ]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Romanian "Help:IPA/Romanian")), **Argeș Pitești** or simply **FC Argeș**, is a Romanian professional [football club](/wiki/Football_club "Football club") based in [Pitești](/wiki/Pite%C8%99ti "Pitești"), [Argeș County](/wiki/Arge%C8%99_County "Argeș County"), that competes in the [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I"), the top tier of [Romanian football](/wiki/Romanian_football_league_system "Romanian football league system").

The team was originally founded as *Dinamo Pitești* in 1953, and made its [top flight](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") debut in the [1961–62 season](/wiki/1961%E2%80%9362_Divizia_A "1961–62 Divizia A"). In 1967, it changed its name to **Argeș Pitești**. The club's most successful period was in the 1970s, when it won the national title twice. Three-time [Romanian Footballer of the Year](/wiki/Romanian_Footballer_of_the_Year "Romanian Footballer of the Year") award recipient [Nicolae Dobrin](/wiki/Nicolae_Dobrin "Nicolae Dobrin") was the most important member of the squad during that period, which turned him into a club icon over the years.

FC Argeș amassed over 40 seasons in the Liga I, and traditionally plays its home matches at [Nicolae Dobrin Stadium](/wiki/Nicolae_Dobrin_Stadium "Nicolae Dobrin Stadium"), which is currently under reconstruction.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

|  |  |
| --- | --- |
| Period | Name |
| 1956–1967 | *Dinamo Pitești* |
| 1967–1992 | *Argeș Pitești* |

### Founding and early years (1953–1967)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Founding and early years (1953–1967)")]

On 6 August 1953, an order of the [Ministry of Internal Affairs](/wiki/Ministry_of_Internal_Affairs_(Romania) "Ministry of Internal Affairs (Romania)") created Dinamo Pitești, the original formation of FC Argeș. The name was based on an older [Bucharest](/wiki/Bucharest "Bucharest") team, [FC Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București"). The new team from [Pitești](/wiki/Pite%C8%99ti "Pitești") started its rise from the bottom, in the City Championship ([Romanian](/wiki/Romanian_language "Romanian language"): *Campionatul Orășenesc*), today [Liga IV](/wiki/Liga_IV "Liga IV") or [Liga V](/wiki/Liga_V "Liga V"), but the involvement of the local administration in bringing the best football players from the city to FC Argeș was the main factor in the consecutive promotions of the club. The promotion to [Divizia B](/wiki/Liga_II "Liga II") (Liga II) occurred at the end of the 1958–1959 [Divizia C](/wiki/Liga_III "Liga III") (Liga III) season, and in the first year the team was very close to a [Divizia A](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") (Liga I) promotion, but ended up in second place of the second series of Divizia B. In the following season, the team was promoted to the top Romanian football league under the command of coaches Ştefan Vasile and Tănase Dima. Three of the most well-known members of the squad were [Ion Barbu](/wiki/Ion_Barbu_(footballer,_born_1938) "Ion Barbu (footballer, born 1938)"), [Florin Halagian](/wiki/Florin_Halagian "Florin Halagian") and [Nicolae Dobrin](/wiki/Nicolae_Dobrin "Nicolae Dobrin"), players who contributed the most to Dinamo's performance. The [1961–62 Divizia A](/wiki/1961%E2%80%9362_Divizia_A "1961–62 Divizia A") season was a tough one for the club which was relegated back to Divizia B after only one year.

After only one season in Divizia B, Dinamo Pitești were promoted again in 1963. They would remain a constant presence on the first stage of Romanian football, finishing 10th place in [1964](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_Divizia_A "1963–64 Divizia A") while also winning the [Romanian Cup](/wiki/Cupa_Rom%C3%A2niei "Cupa României") final, 8th in [1965](/wiki/1964%E2%80%9365_Divizia_A "1964–65 Divizia A"), 4th in [1966](/wiki/1965%E2%80%9366_Divizia_A "1965–66 Divizia A") and 12th in [1967](/wiki/1966%E2%80%9367_Divizia_A "1966–67 Divizia A").[[1]](#cite_note-1)

### Golden era (1967–1983)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Golden era (1967–1983)")]

In the summer of 1967 the club changed its name from Dinamo Pitești to Argeș Pitești. The first season with the new name was a great one for the club, which finished 2nd, with the same number of points as [Steaua București](/wiki/FC_Steaua_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Steaua București"), the champions of that season. It was the best ranking in the club's history until then, but was followed by a 12th place in [1969](/wiki/1968%E2%80%9369_Divizia_A "1968–69 Divizia A"), a 10th place in [1970](/wiki/1969%E2%80%9370_Divizia_A "1969–70 Divizia A") and a 9th place in [1971](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_Divizia_A "1970–71 Divizia A").

In the [1971–72 Divizia A](/wiki/1971%E2%80%9372_Divizia_A "1971–72 Divizia A") season nothing could have predicted the final success of the team. In the first round the team debuted poorly, with a defeat (1–4) at [SC Bacău](/wiki/FCM_Bac%C4%83u "FCM Bacău"), and the tension within the team led to the dismissal of coach [Titus Ozon](/wiki/Titus_Ozon "Titus Ozon"). In his place Florin Halagian was promoted, a young coach and former member of the team. At his debut as the youngest coach of Divizia A on 29 August 1971, the team won 2–1 against [Rapid București](/wiki/FC_Rapid_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Rapid București"), with both goals scored by [Jercan](/wiki/Radu_Jercan "Radu Jercan"). At the end of the first part of the championship, FC Argeş was in 4th place with 18 points, behind [UTA Arad](/wiki/FC_UTA_Arad "FC UTA Arad"), SC Bacău and [Universitatea Cluj](/wiki/FC_Universitatea_Cluj "FC Universitatea Cluj"). The winter preparations took place at [Băile Herculane](/wiki/B%C4%83ile_Herculane "Băile Herculane"), followed by a strong tournament in [East Germany](/wiki/East_Germany "East Germany"), which helped the team a lot in the second part of the championship. In the second part, FC Argeș was defeated only twice (0–2, at home against [Steagul Roșu Brașov](/wiki/FC_Bra%C8%99ov_(1936) "FC Brașov (1936)") and 0–1, at [Cluj-Napoca](/wiki/Cluj-Napoca "Cluj-Napoca"), against [CFR Cluj](/wiki/CFR_Cluj "CFR Cluj")). These were followed by 9 games without defeats, with Halagian playing the high card several times.[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] In the match against [Politehnica Iași](/wiki/FC_Politehnica_Ia%C8%99i_(1945) "FC Politehnica Iași (1945)"), he played each half with a different line of midfielders, to everyone's surprise, to force the victory and to have fresh players until the end. In the penultimate stage on 21 June 1972 at Pitești, FC Argeș defeated [Crişul Oradea](/wiki/FC_Bihor_Oradea_(1958) "FC Bihor Oradea (1958)"), becoming champion of Romania for the first time in its history, with one round before the end of the championship. The team played offensively and ambitiously, with an inspired Dobrin at the helm. The score was 4–1 (3–1) with the goals scored by Dobrin, [Prepurgel](/wiki/Ion_Prepurgel "Ion Prepurgel"), and M. Joita, and Tămaş scoring for Crișul. In front of over 17,000 spectators in the final round, FC Argeș defeated Dinamo București at [23 August Stadium](/wiki/Stadionul_Na%C8%9Bional_(1953) "Stadionul Național (1953)") with a score of 3–2, through the goals scored by Jercan, [Constantin Radu](/wiki/Constantin_Radu_(footballer) "Constantin Radu (footballer)"), and [Frățilă](/wiki/Constantin_Fr%C4%83%C8%9Bil%C4%83 "Constantin Frățilă"), with [Lucescu](/wiki/Mircea_Lucescu "Mircea Lucescu") and [Dumitrache](/wiki/Florea_Dumitrache "Florea Dumitrache") scoring for Dinamo.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

FC Argeș's 1971–72 season line-up

Following their 1972 Divizia A title, FC Argeș played in the [European Champion Clubs' Cup](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League"). In the first round the team eliminated their opponents without difficulty, beating [Aris Bonnevoie](/wiki/FC_Aris_Bonnevoie "FC Aris Bonnevoie") from [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg") 6–0. Then in the second round they faced [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F."), a team with 6 Champions' Cups at the time and top players such as [Pirri](/wiki/Pirri "Pirri"), [Ignacio Zoco](/wiki/Ignacio_Zoco "Ignacio Zoco"), [Santillana](/wiki/Santillana_(footballer) "Santillana (footballer)"), and [Amancio](/wiki/Amancio_Amaro "Amancio Amaro"), a legendary coach [Miguel Muñoz](/wiki/Miguel_Mu%C3%B1oz "Miguel Muñoz") and president [Santiago Bernabéu](/wiki/Santiago_Bernab%C3%A9u_Yeste "Santiago Bernabéu Yeste"). The match seemed like it would be a formality for Real Madrid, but on the pitch FC Argeș made probably the best match in its entire history, one which made Dobrin famous. He scored for 1–0 at 24 minutes, then Anzarda scored at 41 minutes, tying the game. Prepurgel then scored for 2–1 at 62 minutes, followed by several failed scoring opportunities against goalkeeper [García Remón](/wiki/Mariano_Garc%C3%ADa_Rem%C3%B3n "Mariano García Remón"). Bernabéu, amazed by Dobrin's techniques, offered 2 million dollars and to pay for the installations of stadium floodlights for him, but Romanian leader [Nicolae Ceaușescu](/wiki/Nicolae_Ceau%C8%99escu "Nicolae Ceaușescu") refused. At [Madrid](/wiki/Madrid "Madrid"), Real barely qualified with a goal scored at 87 minutes. The final score was 3–1, with Santillana (17 and 87 min) and Grande (47 min) scoring for Real, and [Marin Radu](/wiki/Marin_Radu "Marin Radu") (Radu II) scoring at 43 min for FC Argeș.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The following seasons were oscillating for the team which finished 3rd in [1973](/wiki/1972%E2%80%9373_Divizia_A "1972–73 Divizia A"), 8th in [1974](/wiki/1973%E2%80%9374_Divizia_A "1973–74 Divizia A"), and 7th in [1975](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_Divizia_A "1974–75 Divizia A"), falling to 11th place in [1976](/wiki/1975%E2%80%9376_Divizia_A "1975–76 Divizia A") and [1977](/wiki/1976%E2%80%9377_Divizia_A "1976–77 Divizia A"). In the [1977–78 Divizia A](/wiki/1977%E2%80%9378_Divizia_A "1977–78 Divizia A") season the team had a remarkable comeback and finished 2nd, with the same number of points as the leader, Steaua București, as in 1968.

The [1978–79 Divizia A](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_Divizia_A "1978–79 Divizia A") season began with the sign of good preparation and the desire to complete the maturation of the group of young players, most of whom grew up in Pitești. The team was still under the leadership of Coach Halagian, helped by Leonte lanovschi and [Constantin Oţet](/wiki/Constantin_O%C5%A3et "Constantin Oţet"), who transferred a few days before the start of the championship. The goal of the club's leadership was to occupy one of the top five places and to accumulate at least 18 points in the first championship round. FC Argeș started the championship strong, winning two games against [Chimia Râmnicu Vâlcea](/wiki/Chimia_R%C3%A2mnicu_V%C3%A2lcea "Chimia Râmnicu Vâlcea") and Politehnica Iași and ending up in a leading place. It was only in the 6th round when the club recorded its first defeat, 0–1 at [Târgu Mureș](/wiki/T%C3%A2rgu_Mure%C8%99 "Târgu Mureș") against [ASA Târgu Mureș](/wiki/ASA_T%C3%A2rgu_Mure%C8%99_(1962) "ASA Târgu Mureș (1962)"). The players were subjected to double efforts, most of them being part of the Romanian Olympic team, which managed, after many years, to defeat the Hungarian team at Pitești. At the end of the first round, FC Argeş was in first place. A new player successfully debuted with the team, [Moiceanu](/wiki/Viorel_Moiceanu "Viorel Moiceanu"), who soon became a secret weapon for the team. In the last stage of the first round, FC Argeș won against Dinamo București 1–0, and became the champion of the round with 10 matches won, a draw and 6 defeats, 25 goals scored and 17 conceded, for a total of 21 points, three more than their objective at the start of the championship.

The well-known sports journalist Laurențiu Dumitrescu wrote in the *Sportul* newspaper,

> ... it was also in the autumn of [1978 that] Dobrin's team, the team of that highly talented player, who has been wearing the number 10 shirt for more than 15 years, the player capable – even at 31 years old – of deciding any match in our championship (testimonial examples: matches against [FC Baia Mare](/wiki/FC_Baia_Mare "FC Baia Mare"), on their own ground, and against Steaua, away) to score spectacular goals. When Dobrin was absent, [Iovănescu](/wiki/Sevastian_Iov%C4%83nescu "Sevastian Iovănescu") stepped in, an offensive midfielder who had an exceptional season, perhaps the best of his career. He also assumed the role of *puncheur* when the striker Radu II was injured. But it was also [Stancu](/wiki/Constantin_Stancu "Constantin Stancu")'s turn, who became at only 22 years old, the mature leader of the defense, who combined the experience of [Ivan II](/wiki/Petre_Ivan_(footballer,_born_1946) "Petre Ivan (footballer, born 1946)") with the strength of [Cârstea](/wiki/Constantin_C%C3%A2rstea "Constantin Cârstea") and the ambition of [Mihai Zamfir](/wiki/Mihai_Zamfir_(footballer) "Mihai Zamfir (footballer)").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/22/Nicolae_Dobrin_2.jpg/250px-Nicolae_Dobrin_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:Nicolae_Dobrin_2.jpg)

[Nicolae Dobrin](/wiki/Nicolae_Dobrin "Nicolae Dobrin"), club symbol and inaugural winner of the [Romanian Footballer of the Year](/wiki/Romanian_Footballer_of_the_Year "Romanian Footballer of the Year") award

On 24 June 1979, in front of 20,000 spectators at [Dinamo Stadium](/wiki/Stadionul_Dinamo "Stadionul Dinamo") in Bucharest, FC Argeș defeated Dinamo 4–3 after a high-tension match, through goals scored by Radu II (11 and 24 min), [Doru Nicolae](/wiki/Doru_Nicolae "Doru Nicolae") (69 min) and Dobrin (90 min) respectively, with [Marin Dragnea](/wiki/Marin_Dragnea "Marin Dragnea") (6 min) and [Dudu Georgescu](/wiki/Dudu_Georgescu "Dudu Georgescu") (76 min from penalty and 89 min) scoring for Dinamo. This win brought FC Argeș the Romanian title for the second time in its history.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

After the game, the well-known sports journalist [Ioan Chirilă](/wiki/Ioan_Chiril%C4%83 "Ioan Chirilă") wrote in *Sportul*, "The championship final, awaited with extraordinary interest, which massively reduced the number of spectators on all the other grounds, ended with the well-deserved victory of the Pitești players after a high-tension game, in which the Dynamo team's thirst for victory received a veto from Dobrin, who offered the stands and viewers the countless matches of his life, he being the player who dominated the field from all points of view."[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

In the [1978–79 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1978%E2%80%9379_UEFA_Cup "1978–79 UEFA Cup") FC Argeș eliminated [Panathinaikos](/wiki/Panathinaikos_F.C. "Panathinaikos F.C.") and went down with a fight against [Valencia](/wiki/Valencia_CF "Valencia CF"), with a score of 4–6 on aggregate. The club finished on the podium twice, in [1980](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_Divizia_A "1979–80 Divizia A") and [1981](/wiki/1980%E2%80%9381_Divizia_A "1980–81 Divizia A"), but only in 10th place in [1982](/wiki/1981%E2%80%9382_Divizia_A "1981–82 Divizia A"). Then in the summer of 1982, Dobrin at 35 years old left FC Argeș for [CS Târgoviște](/wiki/FCM_T%C3%A2rgovi%C8%99te "FCM Târgoviște"). The team's performance wasn't greatly impacted and they finished 4th in [1983](/wiki/1982%E2%80%9383_Divizia_A "1982–83 Divizia A"). The next summer Dobrin returned to FC Argeș, but played only 5 matches before retiring on 14 June 1983, after a match against [Bihor Oradea](/wiki/FC_Bihor_Oradea_(1958) "FC Bihor Oradea (1958)"), with the team finishing in 5th place.[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)

### After Nicolae Dobrin (1983–2009)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: After Nicolae Dobrin (1983–2009)")]

The retirement of Dobrin, the emblematic symbol of FC Argeș, also meant the end of great performances for the club. In the 1980s the team became mediocre, finishing frequently in 6th, 7th or 9th place, but still managed to reach three [Balkans Cup](/wiki/Balkans_Cup "Balkans Cup") finals in this period. At the end of the decade and the beginning of the next the team slipped even further, culminating in their relegation from Divizia A in [1992](/wiki/1991%E2%80%9392_Divizia_A "1991–92 Divizia A"), after 29 years spent in the top league of Romanian football.

Relegated to Liga II and with financial problems FC Argeș was taken over by [Dacia](/wiki/Automobile_Dacia "Automobile Dacia"), whose director was Constantin Stroe, who paid the team's debts. The club was promoted back in 1994, finished 8th in [1995](/wiki/1994%E2%80%9395_Divizia_A "1994–95 Divizia A"), but was again 5 points away from relegation in [1996](/wiki/1995%E2%80%9396_Divizia_A "1995–96 Divizia A"). In the next season the team made a comeback to the middle of the standings.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The [1997–98 Divizia A](/wiki/1997%E2%80%9398_Divizia_A "1997–98 Divizia A") season was the last peak of FC Argeș' history. The team finished 3rd and qualified for the [1998–99 UEFA Cup](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_UEFA_Cup "1998–99 UEFA Cup"), where they eliminated teams like [Dynamo Baku](/wiki/FC_Baku "FC Baku") and [İstanbulspor](/wiki/%C4%B0stanbulspor "İstanbulspor") but were easily eliminated by [Celta de Vigo](/wiki/Celta_de_Vigo "Celta de Vigo") 0–8 on aggregate. This last presence in European Cups was due to a formidable generation, the second most talented after Dobrin's. The team included the players [Adrian Mutu](/wiki/Adrian_Mutu "Adrian Mutu"), [Bogdan Vintilă](/wiki/Bogdan_Vintil%C4%83 "Bogdan Vintilă"), [Valentin Năstase](/wiki/Valentin_N%C4%83stase "Valentin Năstase"), [Iulian Crivac](/wiki/Iulian_Crivac "Iulian Crivac"), [Constantin Schumacher](/wiki/Constantin_Schumacher "Constantin Schumacher"), and [Constantin Barbu](/wiki/Constantin_Barbu "Constantin Barbu").[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The team continued its good performance, finishing 4th in [1999](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Divizia_A "1998–99 Divizia A"), then in 5th place in [2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Divizia_A "1999–2000 Divizia A") and [2001](/wiki/2000%E2%80%9301_Divizia_A "2000–01 Divizia A"). From [2002](/wiki/2001%E2%80%9302_Divizia_A "2001–02 Divizia A") on the team was a constant presence in the second half of the leaderboard, finishing at most in 10th place. This period ended with the second relegation in the club's history, this time after 13 years, at the end of the [2006–07 Liga I](/wiki/2006%E2%80%9307_Liga_I "2006–07 Liga I") season. The team was promoted back after only one season in Liga II.

### The Romanian *Calciopoli* and Bankruptcy (2009–2013)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: The Romanian Calciopoli and Bankruptcy (2009–2013)")]

The team finished 8th in their first season after the promotion, a season with good results and a solid playing style. Then on 8 June 2009, they were relegated to Liga II, after the [National Anti-Corruption Division](/wiki/National_Anticorruption_Directorate "National Anticorruption Directorate") found that the owner of the team from that period, Cornel Penescu, tried to bribe referees to benefit the team in matches. This case was called the "Penescu Case" or the "Romanian *[Calciopoli](/wiki/Calciopoli "Calciopoli")*" by the press.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

After Penescu's arrest, his son Andrei led the club, but the funding was practically non-existent and the club struggled to survive even in Liga II, finishing in the second half of the standings. Then on 26 July 2013 it was announced that FC Argeș was bankrupt without any chance of rescue.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### SCM Pitești and supporters' club (2013–2017)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: SCM Pitești and supporters' club (2013–2017)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/64/Scm-logo.png/250px-Scm-logo.png)](/wiki/File:Scm-logo.png)

SCM Pitești logo

After the bankruptcy of FC Argeș, the people of Pitești tried to bring football back to life. Their first project was Sport Club Municipal (SCM) Pitești, a project initiated by Pitești City Council which created a football section at the sporting club of the municipality in 2011, 2 years before the end of FC Argeș, anticipating the bankruptcy of the club led at that time by Penescu's son and burdened with debt. The team was promoted to Liga III after only one season and remained at that level until 2017 when they were promoted to Liga II.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The supporters of FC Argeș encouraged the team until the very end, then after the bankruptcy founded [FC Argeș 1953 Piteşti](/wiki/FC_Arge%C8%99_1953_Pite%C8%99ti "FC Argeș 1953 Pitești"), a [phoenix club](/wiki/Phoenix_club_(sports) "Phoenix club (sports)") fully owned by FC Argeș supporters. The club won the [2015–16 Liga IV](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Liga_IV "2015–16 Liga IV") for Argeș County and the promotion play-off match 5–2 against Recolta Stoicănești, [Olt County](/wiki/Olt_County "Olt County") champion, but were unable to join Liga III due to a lack of funds and dissolved their senior team.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Pitești City Council offered to buy the FC Argeș brand, and the fans running FC Argeș 1953 supported this initiative.[[12]](#cite_note-12)

### Rebirth and return to Liga I (2017–present)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Rebirth and return to Liga I (2017–present)")]

On 16 June 2017, the Municipality of Pitești bought FC Argeș's brand for 550,000 [RON](/wiki/Romanian_leu "Romanian leu").[[13]](#cite_note-13) As a result of this association, the brand was transferred to SCM Pitești's football section,[[14]](#cite_note-14) newly promoted to Liga II, and the team came back to life four years after it was declared bankrupt. After promotion, the team spent 3 years in the second league. FC Argeș managed to earn promotion to the first division at the end of the [2019–20 season](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Liga_II "2019–20 Liga II"), after an 11-year break. The team narrowly earned 2nd place, after Rapid București held [Turris-Oltul Turnu Măgurele](/wiki/Turris-Oltul_Turnu_M%C4%83gurele "Turris-Oltul Turnu Măgurele") to a draw in the seventh minute of extra time, a score at which Turris remained in the second league, and FC Argeș was promoted directly.

In the first half of their [comeback season](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_Liga_I "2020–21 Liga I"), FC Argeș failed to impress, their winless streak leaving them in last place in the league at the beginning of 2021. Things began to change after former player [Andrei Prepeliță](/wiki/Andrei_Prepeli%C8%9B%C4%83 "Andrei Prepeliță") took over as manager, with the team recording an 11-game winning streak and slowly crawling out of the relegation places to join the fight for a play-off spot. With their unbeaten streak cut short by a 0–5 defeat against reigning champions CFR Cluj, FC Argeș kept their momentum but never made it above 7th place. They missed a chance to make it through with a 1–1 draw against fellow play-off contenders [Academica Clinceni](/wiki/FC_Academica_Clinceni "FC Academica Clinceni") and by the penultimate round, they missed play-off qualification altogether following a 1–4 loss against [Hermannstadt](/wiki/FC_Hermannstadt "FC Hermannstadt").

In the [2021–22 season](/wiki/2021%E2%80%9322_Liga_I "2021–22 Liga I"), FC Argeș managed to secure a play-off spot on the final day of the regular season, finishing 4th after a 2–1 away win against [FC Botoșani](/wiki/FC_Boto%C8%99ani "FC Botoșani"), eventually finishing 6th, the team's highest finish in years, the team including players [Alexandru Greab](/wiki/Alexandru_Greab "Alexandru Greab"), [Andrei Tofan](/wiki/Andrei_Tofan "Andrei Tofan"), [Grigore Turda](/wiki/Grigore_Turda "Grigore Turda"), [David Meza](/wiki/David_Meza_(footballer) "David Meza (footballer)") and [Alexandru Ișfan](/wiki/Alexandru_I%C8%99fan "Alexandru Ișfan").[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

In the [2022–23 season](/wiki/2022%E2%80%9323_Liga_I "2022–23 Liga I"), FC Argeș went into chaos, head coach [Andrei Prepeliță](/wiki/Andrei_Prepeli%C8%9B%C4%83 "Andrei Prepeliță") was sacked in October, and the team eventually finished 14th, being relegated after a 8–5 loss on aggregate to [FC Dinamo București](/wiki/FC_Dinamo_Bucure%C8%99ti "FC Dinamo București") in the relegation play-offs, even losing 6–1 in the first leg. Thus a large part of the team's core left. The team continued on the bad way in the [2023–24 season](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_Liga_II "2023–24 Liga II"), finishing 14th.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

The team turned its misfortunes around in the [2024–25 season](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_Liga_II "2024–25 Liga II") under coach [Bogdan Andone](/wiki/Bogdan_Andone "Bogdan Andone"), when the team not only won promotion, but also won the title, returning to [Liga I](/wiki/Liga_I "Liga I") next season.[[18]](#cite_note-18)

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Stadium")]

The club plays its home matches in Nicolae Dobrin Stadium in Pitești. The stadium has a capacity of 15,000 seats, the biggest in Argeș County. The stadium is closed for renovations, so the club plays its matches at the [Orășenesc Stadium](/wiki/Or%C4%83%C8%99enesc_Stadium_(Mioveni) "Orășenesc Stadium (Mioveni)") in [Mioveni](/wiki/Mioveni "Mioveni") at the moment.

## Support

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Support")]

FC Argeș has many supporters in Romania and especially in Argeș County. The [ultra](/wiki/Ultras "Ultras") groups of FC Argeș are *Violet Republic*, *Brigada Vulturii București* and *Frați de weekend*.[[19]](#cite_note-19)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

### Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=FC_Arge%C8%99_Pite%C8%99ti&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

FC Argeș does not have any significant rivalries, but one is with Dinamo București and another is against local team [CS Mioveni](/wiki/CS_Mioveni "CS Mioveni"), with the town of [Mioveni](/wiki/Mioveni "Mioveni") being only 15 kilometres (9.3 mi) away from Pitești.
